facts.label: WHY THEY WEAR ORANGE
facts.story: The Netherlands first wore orange at the 1934 World Cup — orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks — and never switched back. The color owes nothing to the flag, which is red-white-blue; it traces to William of Orange, the 16th-century prince who led the Dutch revolt against Spain and fathered the nation. Ninety years later, the shirt is still called 'Oranje,' a jersey named for a prince who never touched a football, tying every World Cup squad to a royal house four centuries old.

facts.label: CRUYFF'S TWO STRIPES
facts.story: Every teammate wore Adidas' three stripes in 1974, but Johan Cruyff had a personal Puma deal and refused to advertise a rival's logo. The KNVB and Adidas relented, stitching him a one-off shirt with just two stripes down the sleeves, shorts, and socks. Cruyff wore it all the way to the final in Munich, turning a sponsorship dispute into the most recognizable jersey of the tournament — proof that even Total Football's biggest star answered to no one but himself.

1934.headline: One Match, Then Gone
1934.story: Netherlands' first-ever World Cup match was also their last: Kick Smit and Leen Vente both scored in Milan, but Switzerland answered twice more and won 3–2. There was no group stage to fall back on — a single elimination game, a single afternoon, and Oranje's tournament was over before it had really started.

annotations.label: TWO GOALS, ONE EXIT
annotations.story: Kick Smit broke the deadlock in the 29th minute and Leen Vente leveled again in the 69th, but Switzerland answered twice in the final ten minutes to win 3–2 in Milan. There was no group stage cushion — ninety minutes, one match, and Oranje's entire World Cup was finished before the day was.

annotations.label: NO LOGOS, JUST A LION
annotations.story: Long before Adidas stripes or Nike swooshes, the 1934 shirt was unadorned cotton — laced collar, orange body, white shorts, light blue socks, and nothing on the chest but the KNVB's rampant lion crest. Sponsorship on football shirts was still four decades away; this jersey was built for function, not marketing.

annotations.label: AMATEURS WHO EARNED IT
annotations.story: 1934 was the first World Cup that made nations qualify rather than simply inviting them, and the Dutch had to beat Belgium and the Irish Free State in a group just to reach Italy. The entire squad was amateur — clerks and tradesmen who caught trains to matches and were back at their day jobs within the week.

1934.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks

1938.headline: Blanked By Czechoslovakia
1938.story: Another single-elimination format, another early exit. Netherlands couldn't find a way past Czechoslovakia in Le Havre, going scoreless through 90 minutes and extra time before conceding three times late in the game. It remains the only World Cup match in Dutch history in which they failed to score at all.

annotations.label: HELD, THEN IT BROKE
annotations.story: Netherlands matched Czechoslovakia, finalists four years earlier, scoreless through a full ninety minutes in Le Havre. Then extra time turned brutal: Josef Kostálek broke through in the 93rd minute, Oldřich Nejedlý doubled it in the 111th, and Josef Zeman added a third in the 118th. Dutch legs gave out exactly when it mattered most.

annotations.label: THE UNCHANGED JERSEY
annotations.story: The Dutch wore essentially the same kit they'd worn four years earlier in Italy — orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks, laced collar, plain lion crest. In the 1930s, national kits changed by decade rather than by tournament; nobody yet thought to sell supporters a new jersey every summer.

annotations.label: THE LONG SILENCE BEGINS
annotations.story: Nobody in Le Havre knew it, but this defeat would be Netherlands' last World Cup appearance for 36 years. World War II canceled the next two tournaments, and postwar Dutch football stayed amateur and provincial long after professionalism arrived elsewhere — Oranje wouldn't return until Johan Cruyff dragged it back in 1974.

1938.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks

1974.headline: Total Football, Silver Medal
1974.story: Johan Cruyff's Total Football swept through the tournament: Argentina thrashed 4–0, Brazil beaten 2–0 in a second-round decider. Then, in the Munich final, the Dutch scored before West Germany even touched the ball — Johan Neeskens converting a penalty inside two minutes — only to concede twice and lose 2–1, the most painful near-miss in Dutch football history.

annotations.label: THE TURN IS BORN
annotations.story: Trapped on the touchline by Sweden's Jan Olsson, Johan Cruyff feinted a cross, dragged the ball behind his own standing leg, and spun away untouched — a move so unexpected that commentators had no name for it yet. Within days the world did: the Cruyff Turn, invented live at a World Cup, against a defender who never saw it coming.

annotations.label: OUT WITH THE COTTON
annotations.story: The 1974 jersey ditched the heavy fabric of previous decades for a lightweight polyester weave that hugged the body instead of billowing in the wind — a small technical shift that mattered enormously for a team whose entire tactical identity depended on players covering every blade of grass without room to spare.

annotations.label: THE BILD AMBUSH
annotations.story: Five days before the final, German tabloid Bild ran 'Cruyff, Champagne, and Nude Women' — a story about a poolside party at the Dutch team hotel, timed deliberately to rattle the squad before facing the hosts. Wives called the hotel for hours demanding answers. The distraction, several players later admitted, never fully lifted before Munich.

1974.homeColors: orange shirt with black trim, black shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

1978.headline: Nanninga's Header, Argentina's Party
1978.story: Rob Rensenbrink's five group-stage goals carried the Dutch, without an injured Cruyff, to a second straight final, this time in Buenos Aires. Substitute Dick Nanninga headed a late equalizer to force extra time, but Mario Kempes and a deafening home crowd proved too much, and Netherlands fell 3–1 — back-to-back runners-up, still without a trophy.

annotations.label: THE POST, THE FINAL MINUTE
annotations.story: With the final tied 1–1 and seconds left on the clock, Rob Rensenbrink's shot beat Ubaldo Fillol and struck the inside of the post — inches from winning the World Cup outright. It stayed out. Argentina scored twice in extra time, and a shot that grazed metal became the most replayed near-miss in Dutch football history.

annotations.label: TWO STRIPES, NO CRUYFF
annotations.story: Johan Cruyff stayed home in 1978, but his two-stripe Puma shirt didn't disappear with him — twins Rene and Willy van der Kerkhof, plus substitute Dick Nanninga, all wore the non-Adidas two-stripe version throughout the tournament. Crests even flipped sides match to match, a level of kit chaos no modern federation would tolerate.

annotations.label: BOYCOTTING THE PODIUM
annotations.story: Amnesty International ran a global campaign — 'Football Yes, Torture No' — protesting Argentina's military junta hosting the tournament, and the Dutch federation openly debated a boycott before deciding to play. After the final, the squad refused to take part in the medal ceremony rather than share the pitch with General Jorge Videla, walking off before the trophy was lifted.

1978.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

matches.scorers: Brandts 6', Rensenbrink 35', Rep 36', Rep 53', van de Kerkhof 82'

1990.headline: Three Draws, Then Out
1990.story: A flat title defense: three straight 1–1 or scoreless draws left the reigning European champions stumbling into the Round of 16 as one of the better third-placed teams. Ronald Koeman's free kick wasn't enough — West Germany, the eventual champions, eliminated the Dutch 2–1 in Milan, a game remembered for Frank Rijkaard's infamous spit at Rudi Völler.

annotations.label: DECIDED BY A GLASS BOWL
annotations.story: Netherlands and the Republic of Ireland finished dead level after three group games — same points, same goal difference, same 1–1 head-to-head result — so FIFA settled it the only way left: pulling balls from a glass bowl. Sepp Blatter drew the lots live on television, sending Ireland to Romania and the Dutch to a date with West Germany instead.

annotations.label: WHITE WITH AN ORANGE COLLAR
annotations.story: Adidas flipped the formula for the 1990 away kit: crisp white body, but a bold orange fold-down collar and orange stripes across the shoulders, with the KNVB lion crest and Adidas logo both rendered in orange instead of the usual black. It was the boldest inversion of the Oranje template in years — orange as accent, not base.

annotations.label: PLAYERS WANTED CRUYFF
annotations.story: Netherlands arrived as reigning European champions with AC Milan's title-winning trio — Gullit, Van Basten, Rijkaard, nicknamed 'Tre Tulipani' by the Milan crowd — after their first World Cup appearance in twelve years. The squad reportedly wanted Johan Cruyff as manager and got Leo Beenhakker instead; rumors of a rift between Beenhakker and Van Basten shadowed the whole tournament.

1990.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

1994.headline: Bergkamp's Breakout Summer
1994.story: Dennis Bergkamp scored in three straight matches, including the winner over the Republic of Ireland in the Round of 16, announcing himself as a genuine superstar. Brazil, on their way to a fourth title, edged the Dutch 3–2 in a wide-open Dallas quarter-final that came down to who finished their chances better.

annotations.label: THE LEAD THAT VANISHED
annotations.story: Netherlands led Brazil 2–0 in the Dallas quarter-final with under twenty minutes to play, Bergkamp and Winter both scoring. Brazil answered with three goals in nine minutes, the last a rocketed Branco free kick from ten yards outside the box that beat the wall and the goalkeeper both, sending the eventual champions through 3–2.

annotations.label: A LION HIDDEN IN THE FABRIC
annotations.story: Lotto's 1994 shirt buried a subtle lion pattern directly into the orange fabric itself — an all-over embossed print you'd only catch at an angle — paired with a white polo collar striped in red, white, and blue, mirroring the Dutch flag on the cuffs. It was the last Dutch World Cup shirt before Nike took over for good.

annotations.label: GULLIT WALKS AWAY
annotations.story: Weeks before the tournament, Ruud Gullit — the reigning Ballon d'Or winner and captain — abruptly quit the national team in a dispute with coach Dick Advocaat over tactics and playing time, and never played for Oranje again. Netherlands went to the United States without their biggest star and still reached the quarter-finals on Bergkamp's emergence alone.

1994.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Lotto)

1998.headline: Bergkamp's Touch, Then Heartbreak
1998.story: Edgar Davids' 90th-minute winner over FR Yugoslavia and Dennis Bergkamp's sublime touch-and-finish against Argentina sent the Netherlands to the semi-final. Brazil survived on penalties after a 1–1 draw, and a tired Dutch side lost the third-place match to Croatia, closing a heartbreaking summer just short of the podium.

annotations.label: TWO MISSES, ONE OUT
annotations.story: Deadlocked with Brazil 1–1 after 120 minutes in Marseille, the semi-final came down to penalties — and Netherlands missed two of theirs, Phillip Cocu and Ronald de Boer both stopped by Cláudio Taffarel. Brazil converted four straight. It remains the shootout Dutch fans point to as the true final they never got to play.

annotations.label: BLACK PANELS, WHITE CUFFS
annotations.story: The 1998 shirt paired the familiar orange body with black side panels and a black polo collar trimmed in white, plus white cuffs on the sleeves — a sharper, more angular update from the plain jerseys of the early '90s. Edgar Davids wore his with wraparound protective goggles, forced by a detached retina that nearly ended his career two years earlier.

annotations.label: ORANJE'S SURINAMESE CORE
annotations.story: Davids, Seedorf, Kluivert, Reiziger, Winter, Hasselbaink, and Bogarde — seven of the 1998 squad's key contributors traced their roots to Suriname, the former Dutch colony in South America that had gained independence just 23 years earlier. It was the clearest sign yet that post-colonial migration, not just Total Football tactics, had reshaped who wore the Oranje shirt.

1998.homeColors: orange shirt, black shorts, orange socks (Lotto)

matches.scorers: Cocu 37', Overmars 41', Bergkamp 71', van Hooijdonk 80', de Boer 83'

2006.headline: The Battle of Nuremberg
2006.story: A tense, error-strewn 0–0 draw with Argentina set the tone for a knockout meeting with Portugal that turned violent — four red cards and 16 yellows in a game remembered as the Battle of Nuremberg. Maniche's strike was the only goal, and Netherlands went home having played some of the most heavily-officiated football in World Cup history.

annotations.label: NINE MEN STANDING
annotations.story: Referee Valentin Ivanov brandished 20 cards in Nuremberg — a World Cup record that still stands — sending off Portugal's Deco and Costinha and Netherlands' Khalid Boulahrouz and Giovanni van Bronckhorst. By the final whistle both sides were down to nine men, and Maniche's early strike was the only goal a chaotic, stoppage-heavy match ever produced.

annotations.label: THE FLAG, NOT THE CROWN
annotations.story: Nike's 2006 away shirt broke Oranje convention entirely — white base with blue and red trim pulled straight from the Dutch tricolor flag, instead of the royal orange the team had worn since 1907. It was one of the rare Dutch World Cup kits with no orange on it at all.

annotations.label: VAN BASTEN'S FIRST TEST
annotations.story: Marco van Basten, the Ballon d'Or striker of Netherlands' Euro '88 triumph, took charge of the national team for his first major tournament as head coach in 2006. He'd retired from playing at 28 after an ankle injury that never fully healed; twelve years later he was pacing the same touchline as a manager, coaching a golden generation he never got to play alongside.

2006.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)

2010.headline: Iniesta Breaks Dutch Hearts
2010.story: Wesley Sneijder scored five goals to drag the Dutch to their third final, beating Brazil and Uruguay along the way without ever losing control of a match. In Johannesburg, a bruising, foul-riddled final against Spain went scoreless into extra time before Andrés Iniesta struck in the 116th minute, denying Netherlands the trophy yet again.

annotations.label: THE KUNG-FU KICK
annotations.story: Nineteen minutes into the final, Nigel de Jong planted his boot in Xabi Alonso's chest at hip height — a challenge so violent that pundit Ruud Gullit called it 'kung fu' on air. Referee Howard Webb showed yellow instead of red, and the wild, foul-choked final ground on for another 97 minutes before Iniesta finally broke it.

annotations.label: A BLACK V, WHITE STRIPES
annotations.story: Nike's 2010 home shirt kept the orange base but sharpened it with a black V-neck collar and thin white side stripes running the length of the torso — a slimmer, more tailored cut than any previous Oranje shirt, built to look sleek under South Africa's winter floodlights.

annotations.label: THE ORANGE DRESS ARRESTS
annotations.story: During the Denmark group match, security ejected 36 women from the stands, all wearing identical short orange dresses handed out by Dutch brewery Bavaria as guerrilla marketing. Two organizers were later arrested under South African trademark law, prompting the Dutch foreign minister to intervene — a marketing stunt that briefly became a bigger story than the match itself.

2010.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)
2010.awayColors: blue shirt, blue shorts, blue socks (Nike)

2014.headline: Revenge Rout, Bronze Reward
2014.story: Louis van Gaal's side opened by dismantling defending champions Spain 5–1 — Robin van Persie's diving header the highlight — and rolled unbeaten into the semi-final before penalty shootouts eliminated them against Costa Rica and then Argentina. A 3–0 win over Brazil in the third-place match delivered Netherlands' first World Cup bronze.

annotations.label: THE GOALKEEPER GAMBLE
annotations.story: With the Costa Rica quarter-final locked at 0–0 after 120 scoreless minutes, Louis van Gaal used his final substitution to send on backup goalkeeper Tim Krul for the shootout alone — a mind-game no manager had dared on this stage before. Krul saved two penalties, from Bryan Ruiz and Michael Umaña, and Netherlands advanced 4–3.

annotations.label: A TYPOGRAPHER'S NUMBERS
annotations.story: For the KNVB's 125th anniversary, Nike commissioned Dutch typographer Wim Crouwel to design the shirt's names and numbers from scratch, blending a modern font with the blocky look of 1970s numerals. The crest got an overhaul too: a bigger, bolder white lion replacing the small crowned lion head that had been used since the '70s.

annotations.label: COACHING FOR TWO CLUBS
annotations.story: Louis van Gaal had already been unveiled as Manchester United's next manager before the tournament even kicked off, meaning he spent the entire World Cup as a lame-duck national coach with his next job locked in. It didn't stop him from engineering the bronze medal — Netherlands' best finish outside their three runner-up campaigns — before flying to Manchester.

2014.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)
2014.awayColors: blue shirt with orange trim, blue shorts (Nike)

matches.scorers: van Persie 44', Robben 53', de Vrij 64', van Persie 72', Robben 80'

2022.headline: Weghorst's Double, Messi's Answer
2022.story: Cody Gakpo scored in three straight group games to introduce himself to the world, and Denzel Dumfries capped a 3–1 Round of 16 win over the United States. Wout Weghorst's stoppage-time double forced extra time against Argentina in the quarter-final, but Messi's side prevailed on penalties, ending Van Gaal's second spell in charge.
2022.homeColors: orange shirt, black shorts, black socks (Nike)
